text: Standard libraries: We have an stdlib for Swift (where the Dictionary and List are value types and are based on the target frameworks List / Dictionary, so System.Collections.Generic.List<T> on .NET, java.lang.ArrayList<T> on Java, NSMutableArray<T> on Cocoa, etc) , for Go we have a "Slice" type that is convertable to a regular platform array. In the same project you can mix and match languages yes, and trivlally call between things as if they were written in the same language.
We offer three ides actually, our Windows IDE, Cocoa IDE and we integrate into VS

<@UD6EXQVM0> I was doing the usual drill C/C++ to WebAssembly but did not want to use emscripten, so I had to learn almost everything from scratch and I now know how to set up minimal applications meant to be run on the browser. In the end I had built something like an amateur clone of emscripten haha. I agree with you that in general WebAssembly is missing a lot of cool things, would love to see it improve with the years.

Hello, I’m the CEO of <http://www.hyperfiddle.net/> which is a FOC startup currently based in Philadelphia. Hyperfiddle basically takes the Clojure/Datomic information model and extends it through to user interfaces. While the technology is interesting, we think a large part of the work is forming a strategy to get adoption at scale (a smooth path from 0 to 1 to 100 to 100k) and also figuring out an economic model to fund the work.

We are bullish on Clojure because Clojure satisfies the criteria of “smooth adoption path” and has a lot of deeply interesting properties, such as the cost of porting Clojure to a new platform is within about a man-year of effort. Clojure on N platforms makes it possible to build distributed systems across platforms with shared information model, data structures and code. A unified/holistic model. So while Clojure is certainly not perfect, it is designed for real-world usage and makes practical compromises to achieve growth. We see Hyperfiddle as just one component of a self-organizing movement which is larger than any one project or company funding 100% of the work.

What was the motivation for avoiding emscripten? Me, I found it to be a pretty crufty, annoying tool that produces bloated output... did you write about your experience or have you seen a well-written post about using Wasm without emscripten?

The motivation was that I was writing very simple programs, i.e. some pure functions with no dependencies. So the only thing I plugged in are pretty much a malloc and some strncpy/strlen equivalents. Dropping emscripten for that kind of thing was overkill, also I always want to learn how things work from scratch. I generally dislike "frameworks" built on top of other things (jQuery, express, you name it).

I have a very dogmatic approach to software development where I want to keep things minimal and with a "bootstrap yourself" attitude. Of course it's (only a bit) more painful but you actually understand the nuts and bolts of everything and that gives you some kind of "magic" instinct when you are solving problems with that particular tool.

Side note: which ironically has prevented me from landing some jobs as they want the guy that works with the framework. I tell them, I can learn your framework in one afternoon since it was built on the actual things that I understand 100%. But the answer is usually "yeah no". Then they go hire the guy who is an "expert" with laravel (or django, or <whatever_tool> bla bla) but spends their time on stackoverflow asking things like "What is a network port?"

<@UD6EXQVM0> But back to your other question >"have you seen a well-written post about using Wasm without emscripten?" Actually it was a pain in the ass to actually figure out what you should do and what you shouldn't. There's like 5 or 6 posts around the net with instructions but not one of them actually works good. There is even one gist with step by step instructions that doesn't work at all if you follow them, because of some wrong syntax when calling clang. That thing should had never worked for anyone yet the gist is full of "thanks!" "this is great" comments from other developers from an alternate universe where clang actually works with these made up parameters :^). But (again) good knowledge of the C standard and compilers (clang, GCC) helped me a lot,since the tricky part (for me) was compiling the actual .wasm without linking and keeping only the functions I wanted.
<@UD6EXQVM0> If you have specific questions about that I'll be glad to help you.

"I have a very dogmatic approach ... keep things minimal ... bootstrap yourself"

Sounds like a good fit for this community!

Have you explored (or are you interested in) anything along the lines of bootstrapping your own approach to software?

For example, I'm exploring an approach wherein the program being created, the process for building that program from source "code" (e.g. the "language" and "compiler"), and the UI for viewing and editing all of these things, ... is all defined within a single self-contained tool, capable of editing/molding it itself (or its contents) on the fly. The idea is that you program using whatever language (or other representation/visualization), tools, and process you like.

The most related thing I've done is figuring out how to compile C code on the fly and loading it at runtime (using Bellard's TCC and again only simple and pure functions). What you suggest is pretty cool in the way that it sounds like it would allow for this kind of thing to be implemented effortlessly. I would love to take a look at it when it's ready :smile:.

attachments.title: Curtis Yarvin
attachments.text: Curtis Guy Yarvin (born June 25, 1973), also known by the pen name Mencius Moldbug, is an American political theorist and computer scientist. Writing in his blog "Unqualified Reservations," he played a fundamental role in the Dark Enlightenment and Alt-right movement. He is the creator of the Urbit computing platform, through his startup company Tlon, which is backed by Peter <http://Thiel.In|Thiel.In> the book Alt-Right: From 4Chan to the White House, Mike Wendling called Yarvin "the Alt right’s favorite philosophy instructor." He added, "Yarvin’s key contribution to the development of alt-right thought was a searing critique of democracy based on supposed genetic 'facts' combined with a dash of intellectual snobbery."

text:
> One main question I am currently focusing on is: What is a (computer) program?

Brilliant question. To me the program concept is turning out be something of a diversion. We think we work with one program mostly but in reality we work with a cacophony of them and other non-programmy things (shared libraries - are they inside my program or outside?). Roughly a program seems like a static description of some automated **process**. I'd argue that the processes of interest mostly span slices across multiple such definitions. I'm trying to focus away from the idea of a program and instead think about the larger context of the 'system'. See <https://shalabh.com/programmable-systems/systems-not-programs.html>

attachments.title: Systems, not Programs
attachments.text: We need programming, but do we need the program?

text:
That HN comment is completely off-base IMO. There was and still is plenty of money made with non web software so the premise itself is shaky.

Further, I don't see why a subscription based model is at odds with the local-first idea. If anything, it appears that many workable business models might work successfully with local-first. A trivial one that comes to mind is based on the app-store model - your data is local-first but operated on by 'apps' you buy (or subscribe to), download and run locally.

I’m looking forward to your presentation in <#CBC968C1M|nyc>!

<@UHS9K1N6A> “tables are not a fundamental construct in real-world data”. Interesting—why do you say so? I’d be inclined to say the opposite, that tables/dataframes are a better fit to most real-world data than any other programming language primitive (lists, sets, sets, tuples, records). Of course, a table is isomorphic to a list of records or a list of tuples, but I think it’s telling that pandas or DataFrames.jl or the R language try to make the table a first-class citizen.

“tables do not have first-class references”—yes, traditionally, foreign-key relationships have been up to the query author to interpret manually via joins. There’s no reason those references can’t be first-class though, other than tradition. The reason I’m pushing back is because I’m hard-pressed to think of a real-world scenario that’s better modeled using nested data rather than some form of normalized table structure that represents the same relationship graph. Back from my web-dev days, I can count on a single hand the number of times I used the JSON cell type in Postgres—it was always some unusual use case. The problem with a nested data model is that the nesting necessarily prioritizes one side of the relationship. Consider the classic example of “Users” and “Posts”.  In a nested model, you’d nest the posts under users; but that means to get all the posts to filter, sort, etc, you necessarily have to look at all the users! In pseudocode: map each user to users.posts, flatten the lists, then filter. Normalized tables let you treat both Users and Posts as first class entities: you can query each without necessarily having to route through the other.

Graphs also have no notion of “normalization” i think, which is because graphs are universal and always normal, tables are derived on top of graphs and at this point adds in a bunch of decision points of which columns go in what table, join tables for many-many, etc
